name: LLM Proxy Student Reliability Is Tested on a Shared IRT Ability Scale
statement: Srivatsa et al. evaluate LLM proxy students by mapping model and real-student response patterns onto the same IRT ability scale, finding that no tested model-prompt combination reliably simulates real student ability patterns across subjects and grades.
population_or_context: NAEP mathematics and reading comprehension item responses.
quantitative_note: The ACL Anthology abstract reports 489 NAEP items, thousands of real student responses, and 11 LLMs tested with several prompting strategies.
caveats: The finding is a negative reliability result for proxy-student ability simulation.
The evaluation is psychometric and item-response based rather than dialogue or essay realism based.
evaluation_method: IRT-scale comparison of model response patterns and real student response patterns across subjects and grades.
reported_metrics: IRT ability-scale alignment across 489 NAEP items, 11 LLMs, and multiple prompting strategies.
limitations: The result is bounded to NAEP mathematics and reading comprehension items.
IRT ability alignment does not directly measure open-response realism, naturalness, or downstream scorer utility.
The top-line abstract conclusion should be refined with full table extraction before model-specific claims.

source_locator.section: ACL Anthology abstract, methodology, and results summary
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The staged finding paraphrases the paper's NAEP item set, real-response comparator, 11-model setup, IRT comparison method, and negative reliability conclusion.
